editorNotes:
Mimikyu's special ability blocks the first unshielded Charged Attack it receives; its Defense then lowers by 1 stage the rest of the match, even if it swaps out.

This effectively grants Mimikyu a third shield, a massive advantage that far outweighs its drawbacks. It performs best when given shields to delay the debuff.

Even with Shadow Sneak and middling stats, Mimikyu's head-to-head matchups are head and shoulders above anything else in PvP. Its neutral typing and Ghost/Fairy coverage further help it excel as a generalist. The only thing reducing its score is that it's an unknown commodity - we'll see if it has the team success it does in the sims.

Lickilicky is the meta's top Normal type, with excellent bulk, a spammy moveset, and powerful Shadow Ball coverage. It's a premier safe swap and a devastating counter to Ghost types in a meta where it has few hard counters to fear.
Tinkaton is a top meta pick with its immensely powerful signature move, Gigaton Hammer. Its biggest drawback is its subpar coverage in Bulldoze, which lends a bait-dependent playstyle.

Altaria's flexibility with Flamethrower gives it coverage against Steel types, and its defensive typing remains one of the best to counter Water and Ground types. It's seeing more play this season with the nerf to its nemesis, Wigglytuff.

Empoleon is the standout Hydro Cannon user thanks to its incredible pacing, Steel typing, and synergy with core partners like Altaria. Its lower stat product and struggles into other Water types haven't hindered its competitive success. The non-Shadow is typically preferred.

Altaria's flexibility with Flamethrower gives it coverage against Steel types, and its defensive typing remains one of the best to counter Water and Ground types. It's seeing more play this season with the nerf to its nemesis, Wigglytuff.

Empoleon is the standout Hydro Cannon user thanks to its incredible pacing, Steel typing, and synergy with core partners like Altaria. Its lower stat product and struggles into other Water types haven't hindered its competitive success. The non-Shadow is typically preferred.

Quagsire has the best bulk and pacing of its competition, making it a top meta pick. Its moveset has shifted to Mud Bomb to hit Steel types, but leaves it more vulnerable to Flying. The Shadow is typically preferred.

Quagsire has the best bulk and pacing of its competition, making it a top meta pick. Its moveset has shifted to Mud Bomb to hit Steel types, but leaves it more vulnerable to Flying. The Shadow is typically preferred.

Jellicent has everything you look for in a top Pokemon - excellent stats, typing, pacing, and power. Its diverse resistances plus the relative scarcity of Grass and Electric make it a centerpiece for team building. Its biggest flaw is its vulnerability to Normal types. As such, Jellicent sees more play in GBL than tournaments.

Forretress has solidified itself as an frontrunner in competitive play with great stats, typing, and attacks. It even has coverage into its solitary weakness. Its playstyle is still on the slower side. Shadow is preferred over non-Shadow.

Kanto Ninetales has quickly risen as a top pick in the new meta. With an improved Energy Ball, it not only fights back against abundant Water types but sometimes straight up wins. Ninetales' new moveset struggles against Dragons and rival Fire types, so you'll need to build your team accordingly.

Kanto Ninetales has quickly risen as a top pick in the new meta. With an improved Energy Ball, it not only fights back against abundant Water types but sometimes straight up wins. Ninetales' new moveset struggles against Dragons and rival Fire types, so you'll need to build your team accordingly.

Forretress has solidified itself as an frontrunner in competitive play with great stats, typing, and attacks. It even has coverage into its solitary weakness, but has more to fear now with Kanto Ninetales on the scene. Shadow is preferred over non-Shadow with both Bug Bite and Volt Switch as viable options.

Despite significant meta threats and a deep roster of Ghost types, Galarian Corsola remains a haunting staple. Its bulk is offset by a somewhat sluggish and underpowered moveset. A high rank Galarian Corsola has better neutral matchups than one with hatch IV's, so look to save your resources for the right one.

Feraligatr is a longtime staple thanks to its powerful moveset. Shadow Claw shreds through ever-present Ghost types, and gives it a more neutral profile into other Waters. Preference for Shadow vs non-Shadow is split, currently leaning toward non-Shadow.

Jumpluff is a standout winner of the latest updates. With faster access to Energy Ball, it can safely run its STAB moveset instead of its old dual-Flying set. Combine that with its extremely bulky stats, and Jumpluff is a rare Grass type with relevance in the open meta. The non-Shadow is generally preferred since its moves are higher energy.

Clodsire is finding its way back into the meta after a brief hiatus. It's always been a solid generalist. Its main drawbacks are its low Fast Attack pressure and its weaknesses to Ground, Ice, and Water.

Corviknight has offensive struggles, but is a cornerstone for competitive play. Its defensive profile makes it a prime team player, especially alongside classic Water/Ground types. Payback is valuable in the mirror matchup, and to chunk meta Ghost types.
Fearow's solid pacing and great offenses bring it to the forefront this season. While its bulk is lacking, its Normal typing is highly valuable against Great League's resident Ghost types.

Feraligatr is a longtime staple thanks to its powerful moveset. Shadow Claw shreds through ever-present Ghost types, and gives it a more neutral profile into other Waters. Preference for Shadow vs non-Shadow is split, currently leaning toward non-Shadow.

Kingdra is on the outside looking in with its new move, Surf. Its typing gives it a neutral profile. It sits ahead of pure Dragon types like Dragonair, but behind the frontrunner, Altaria, whose bulk and coverage are a big step up. Kingdra's niche is its dominating matchups against Fire types, which it double resists.

Azumarill has withstood the test of time and remains one of Great League's most prominent mascots. It owes this to its extreme bulk and excellent defensive typing. Azumarill's main drawback is its pacing, which leaves it outsped by the format's more aggressive picks.

Guzzlord is one of the most viable Dragon types behind Altaria. Its Dark typing gives it the edge against Ghosts and popular Normal types like Lickilicky and Furret. With Wigglytuff nerfed, it has at least one less nightmare matchup to worry about. Guzzlord's insanely high stamina stat makes it resilient to low-damage Fast Attacks.

Shadow Corviknight makes up some of the normal form's offensive shortcomings. Sand Attack breakpoints in particular tangibly improve its ability to farm down and close games. For those who find regular Corviknight lacking, Shadow Corviknight is worth serious consideration.

Furret sits behind Lickilicky in the Normal category, but is still plenty capable. It's slower and less bulky, but offers Fast Attack pressure and coverage into Water types. It's also another notable hard counter to Mimikyu, whose debut could help Furret see even more play.

Kingdra is on the outside looking in with its new move, Surf. Its typing gives it a neutral profile. It sits ahead of pure Dragon types like Dragonair, but behind the frontrunner, Altaria, whose bulk and coverage are a big step up. Kingdra's niche is its dominating matchups against Fire types, which it double resists.

Lapras has a long competitive history, but is currently on its way out of the metagame. It's a poster child for bulky Water types and neutral play. Recent shifts have left it without a clear role, and it now faces stiff competition among Water/Ice types.

Dragonair is a fast-paced, aggressive pick. Despite its offensive strengths, it falls short of rival Dragons like Altaria, Guzzlord, and Kingdra in terms of bulk and resistances. Because of this, it hasn't seen widespread play in a while, but watch for it in Dragon-centered metas. The Shadow is almost always preferred.

Shadow Sableye has recently emerged as a surprising and powerful anti-meta pick with Drain Punch, of all things. Its fast-paced Defense boost, now with real damage, gives Sableye even more neutral play. This moveset is aggressive and technical, and requires significant practice to know which move to throw when.

Seismitoad provides a shakeup as a new Water/Ground contender. It's neither the bulkiest or hardest hitting in that category, but it makes up for it in the sheer utility of Icy Wind. With a steady 5 pacing, Icy Wind is a direct threat to its only weakness (Grass), and throws a wrench into popular team cores. Look for Seismitoad to become firmly anti-meta, if not meta outright.

Umbreon stands among the bulkiest Pokemon in the format. While its top meta days are far, far behind it, Umbreon has improved potential with the nerf to Charm. Its biggest draw is its ability to absorb damage and grind matches to a halt, serving as a pivot or tempo reset. Its offenses are completely lacking, so pair it with high pressure picks for the most synergy.

Medicham may not be at its previous heights, but it's seen some renewed play, especially with the narrow Fighting roster. Its struggles against Ghost and Fairy remain, and its neutral matchups aren't as prolific as players remember.

Outside of the simulations, Talonflame is one of the format's most dangerous sweepers. It shines with a shield advantage. Its 5-turn Fast Attack gives it a steep learning curve, difficult to master and exploitable by skilled opponents. However, these haven't deterred its high usage.

Dragonair is a fast-paced, aggressive pick. Despite its offensive strengths, it falls short of rival Dragons like Altaria, Guzzlord, and Kingdra in terms of bulk and resistances. Because of this, it hasn't seen widespread play in a while, but watch for it in Dragon-centered metas. The Shadow is almost always preferred.

Similar to Greedent, Oinkologne has all of the makings of a solid Pokemon but is overshadowed by LIckilicky. Female Oinkologne has more defensive base stats than Male Oinkologne, so it's strictly better for PvP.

From zero to hero, Sliggoo boasts the highest stat product among pure Dragon types. While it's certainly capable, pure Dragon types don't typically see much play over dual-type alternatives like Altaria or Guzzlord. Sliggoo also lacks corebreaking coverage which made Goodra and Shelgon interesting in the past. All in all, it has a few things to prove before it can fully claim hero status.
Sealeo has the offenses and pacing to make for a neutral, energy-oriented threat. It struggled to make headway for a while, but has found recent success, being favored over Lapras.
Greedent has a lot of things going for it, including stats and moves. While it's plenty viable, it's overshadowed by stronger Normal types like Lickilicky.

Grumpig has recently worked its way into the metagame despite some previous shortcomings. While its stats and typing are nothing to write home about, its Ghost + Fighting offenses are enough to make it a flexible choice, especially for tournament play.
Sealeo has the offenses and pacing to make for a neutral, energy-oriented threat. It struggled to make headway for a while, but has found recent success, being favored over Lapras.

Diggersby is reemerging to contend with rising Steel picks, particularly Forretress. It's not best in class in either the Normal or Ground departments, but with its sheer bulk, it shouldn't be underestimated. The regular version is preferred over Shadow.

Dusclops has been largely replaced by Jellicent, who has stronger offenses and the benefits of Water typing. Dusclops is still viable in its own right, but much less common than alternative Ghosts, including its evolution, Dusknoir.

Florges's mix of coverage gives it a unique matchup profile and technical play. Without Disarming Voice, it can be awkward into Dragons. Recent changes have pushed Florges down the Fairy pecking order, but it can still succeed if you're looking for a disruptive playstyle.

Lapras has a long competitive history, but is currently on its way out of the metagame. It's a poster child for bulky Water types and neutral play. Recent shifts have left it without a clear role as it lacks decisive matchups in the meta.

Sableye is a longtime staple which has phased in and out of the recent meta. Wigglytuff and Tinkaton are severe threats, so its traditional role as a safe swap is no longer safe. Still, its flexibility always offers a high degree of play. Return can't be regained once it's removed, so carefully consider your options before you TM it away.

Wigglytuff has been nerfed, but is it gone for good? While its neutral matchups are dialed back, the hard counter role it's known for still remains. Its pacing to Swift and Icy Wind are also unchanged. Given its history and infamy, Wigglytuff is still likely to see play and shouldn't be counted out.

Cradily isn't fully extinct, but nerfs and meta changes have pushed it to the fringe. Cradily thrives by piling Attack drops onto its natural bulk. It works best in the backline where it can make those Attack drops stick. Cradily isn't the fastest, so shields are important to block early damage.

Dusclops has been largely replaced by Jellicent, who has stronger offenses and the benefits of Water typing. Dusclops is still viable in its own right, but much less common than alternative Ghosts, including its evolution, Dusknoir.

Galarian Moltres has seen better days, with a mix of meta developments pushing it out of the top Flying spot. Its stats are more tempered than Mandibuzz, so it has to watch for coverage and commit to shields.

Blastoise is the bulkiest Hydro Cannon user and can augment this even further with Skull Bash. With an excellent balance of stats, power, and speed, it occupies a small but strong spot in the metagame.
Meteor Mash gives Clefable strong play into other Fairies. However, it faces stiff competition and usually takes a backseat to more well rounded options.

Shadow Talonflame takes its sweeper role to even scarier heights. It occupies a middle ground between regular Talonflame and Shadow Charizard, with a greater focus on Fast Attack pressure and Attack boosts. The simulations more readily capture its pitfalls, where it's harshly punished by Rock, Water, and Electric.

Mandibuzz has sheer bulk on its side, but has failed to break back into the meta in a meaningful way. Its new moveset is walled by rival Dark types, and it struggles significantly against dominant Fairies.

Dewgong has a long competitive history thanks to its bulk and Icy Wind debuffs. It typically underperforms in the sims, where its full game effects aren't well represented. A reworked Drill Run further improves its pacing and damage output, making Dewgong a prospect to watch for.

True to its name, Shadow Steelix is among the standout Steel types in the format. Thunder Fang plus debuffing attacks give it a unique role. The Shadow boost helps make up for its lack of STAB, but it still struggles with its minimal shield pressure and closing power. Look to pair it with strong offensive pressure and Pokemon that can capitalize on alignment.

Charjabug boasts decent stats and neutral play, which are hard to find among other Electric types. Its Bug typing softens its matchups against Ground types, and X-Scissor is highly valuable against Grass. Crunch is an option to hit back against the meta's prominent Ghosts.
Drapion sports neutral typing and a signature fast pace. Prominent Steel types keep it from achieving its past potential. Shadow Drapion is typically preferred over the normal version.
Gastrodon's role has been (water) absorbed by Quagsire. The buff to Earth Power slightly improves its pacing, but won't elevate Gastrodon to previous levels.

The sims don't always use Chilling Water optimally, so Alolan Ninetales should perform better in some head to head matchups than indicated. Chilling Water is a sidegrade, if not a slight improvement which gives Alolan Ninetales coverage against Fire and broader technical play.

The sims don't always use Chilling Water optimally, so Alolan Ninetales should perform better in some head to head matchups than indicated. Chilling Water is a sidegrade, if not a slight improvement which gives Alolan Ninetales coverage against Fire and broader technical play.
Swampert carries a winning legacy since the earliest days of PvP. It has some renewed play, but is firmly behind Quagsire in usage.

It's taken a while, but players are finally finding success with Aegislash in competitive. Its unique mechanics can feel clunky and take dedication to master. Mimikyu's debut hampers Aegislash viability, as Aegislash is completely thwarted by it. Before powering up, make sure to check your Aegislash's IV ranks in both forms.

Bastiodon is the sheer definition of bulk. It has two double weaknesses to crack it, and its abysmal pacing and pressure can leave it exposed in bad situations. That said, Bastiodon's playstyle is not for the faint of heart. Good predictions and commitment to alignment are keys for success.

Shadow Marowak faces a Mud Slap nerf and more Flying types this season, putting it on the backfoot. It has weathered multiple meta updates in the past, so time will tell if it hangs on. The Shadow version is almost always preferred.
Swampert carries a winning legacy since the earliest days of PvP. It has some renewed play, but is firmly behind Quagsire in usage.

Registeel is a relic from the ancient past. Its stats are still legendary, but it now faces a much stronger field. Registeel's complete lack of Fast Attack pressure means it must rely on Charged Attacks to KO, making it challenging to play. While it has some promising matchups, skepticism is warranted as modern meta picks can dance in circles around it.

Hydreigon has the typing to corebreak current meta threats. However, its double weakness to Fairy and glassy stats pose severe challenges. Expert hands may be required to pilot this one over more established picks like Guzzlord.

Turtonator's excellent typing and solid attacks have yet to materialize any competitive success. While it can excel in themed cups, it faces too many challenges in open Great League, especially against prevalent Water types.

Oranguru has solid stats and a neutral type profile. Confusion gives it a clunky playstyle. It's frequently a top pick in themed cups, but doesn't see play in open competition, where Psychic damage is less valuable and it's eclipsed by other Normal types.

Gourgeist is an alternate Grass option with its truly unique moveset. As a Ghost, it also operates as a solid generalist. However, key Normal types fill the generalist role better and threaten Gourgeist in return. Gourgeist works best as a surprise corebreaker with ample  support against Normal and Flying.

Gourgeist is an alternate Grass option with its truly unique moveset. As a Ghost, it also operates as a solid generalist. However, key Normal types fill the generalist role better and threaten Gourgeist in return. Gourgeist works best as a surprise corebreaker with ample  support against Normal and Flying.

Gourgeist is an alternate Grass option with its truly unique moveset. As a Ghost, it also operates as a solid generalist. However, key Normal types fill the generalist role better and threaten Gourgeist in return. Gourgeist works best as a surprise corebreaker with ample  support against Normal and Flying.

Hydreigon has the typing to corebreak current meta threats. However, its double weakness to Fairy and glassy stats pose severe challenges. Expert hands may be required to pilot this one over more established picks like Guzzlord.

Doublade has its share of doubters. Despite Shadow Claw, Sacred Sword, and amazing typing, Doublade has yet to prove its sum is greater than the parts. Compared to Aegislash, it's more reliable and less technical, but its higher floor also comes with a lower ceiling in terms of impact.

Samurott looks great on paper, and while it's by no means a bad pick, it lacks the competitive success and neutral play of its peers. Its Bug offenses are its main detriment and are widely resisted by the main meta players.

Crustle is powerhouse in themed cups, but has yet to see competitive play in open Great League. It struggles significantly against the meta's most prevalent Ghost, Steel, and Water types. Charjabug are Forretress are successful alternatives in its role.

Gourgeist is an alternate Grass option with its truly unique moveset. As a Ghost, it also operates as a solid generalist. However, key Normal types fill the generalist role better and threaten Gourgeist in return. Gourgeist works best as a surprise corebreaker with ample  support against Normal and Flying.
Carbink's bulk and Fairy typing aren't enough to carry it to meta relevancy. It rarely sees play over its Steel-type counterpart, Bastiodon, and shares many similar pitfalls with fewer resistances.

Crustle is powerhouse in themed cups, but has yet to see competitive play in open Great League. It struggles significantly against the meta's most prevalent Ghost, Steel, and Water types. Charjabug are Forretress are successful alternatives in its role.

Walrein has a notable competitive past, but hasn't found a way to crack the current metagame. Its time may come again, but faces too much competition against rival Water types, including its pre-evolution, Sealeo.

Once a dreaded Great League staple, Lickitung is viable but outclassed by other Normal types, including its evolution, Lickilicky. Lickitung has high bulk but lacks the speed and power to compete in a meta that is becoming increasingly faster.

Araquanid has a notable competitive history, but it's marred by inconsistency and inflexibility. It relies on Bubble Beam debuffs to chew through matchups, giving it an awkward and sluggish playstyle.

Regidrago packs a powerful signature attack and a spammy neutral option in Vise Grip. Its stats leave something to be desired, and it is more one-dimensional than other Dragon options. It is largely unproven in competition.

Altaria is the only Dragon to see widespread play in the current meta, and Goodra presents few advantages over it. Its middling stats and non-STAB attacks give it a soft matchup profile that allows it to play as a corebreaker, but it it lacks the strength as a generalist to truly succeed.
Shadow Steelix is typically preferred over the regular variant for additional Fast Attack pressure and the boost to its non-STAB attacks.

Spiritomb sees far less play than its counterpart, Sableye. Its pacing is considerably slow in a meta that is growing ever faster. In particular, this means it can't take full advantage of Rock Tomb's debuffs.
Guzzlord is preferred over Zweilous with its slightly better stats and Poison coverage into Fairy counters.

Araquanid has a notable competitive history, but it's marred by inconsistency and inflexibility. It relies on Bubble Beam debuffs to chew through matchups, giving it an awkward and sluggish playstyle. The Shadow version may improve on its Fast Attack pressure, but Bug offenses are still undesirable.
The Shadow variant is strongly preferred, as its Fast Attack pressure can bulldoze through even negative matchups.
Guzzlord is preferred over Zweilous with its slightly better stats and Poison coverage into Fairy counters.

Walrein has a notable competitive past, but hasn't found a way to crack the current metagame. Its time may come again, but faces too much competition against rival Water types, including its pre-evolution, Sealeo.
Metang has good stats and pacing, but has struggled to find competitive success. It struggles significantly against rival Steel types, and its Psychic typing makes it vulnerable to prevalent Ghosts.
Metang has good stats and pacing, but has struggled to find competitive success. It struggles significantly against rival Steel types, and its Psychic typing makes it vulnerable to prevalent Ghosts.
